nativeRange.habitat: Grows on rocks and submerged wood along the Rio Cristalino in Mato Grosso, in the southern Amazon basin, typically in clear, soft, slow-moving blackwater streams shaded by dense rainforest canopy

decorPreferences: fine gravel or sand for easy root penetration
root tabs every 2-3 months in inert substrates
open foreground/midground space for lateral spreading

summary: A hardy, bushy Brazilian foreground/midground creeper from the Rio Cristalino, popularised by Tropica around 2009. Compact green leaves on stiff stems spread by lateral shoots, making a tidy carpet that is generally considered easier than Glossostigma or HC Cuba.
careNotes: Staurogyne repens is a compact, bushy stem plant native to Brazil's Rio Cristalino (Mato Grosso, southern Amazon basin), where it grows on rocks and woody debris along clear forest streams. Tropica is widely credited with popularising the species in the aquarium hobby after introducing it commercially around 2005 (and broadly through their 1-2-Grow! tissue-culture line by ~2009), and it remains one of the most reliable bushy foreground/midground creepers for planted tanks. Mature stems generally stay around 4-10 cm tall with small, vibrant green leaves arranged densely along stiff stems; rather than growing upright like a typical stem plant, it sends out horizontally creeping lateral shoots that knit together into a low, dense mat. It is generally considered easier than other carpet contenders like Glossostigma elatinoides or Hemianthus callitrichoides ('HC Cuba') because it tolerates a wider range of light and CO2 conditions and is slower-growing, requiring less aggressive trimming. Lighting recommendations across sources range from low-moderate to medium-high (roughly 30-60 PAR at substrate level); medium light produces dense, compact growth, while low light works but yields a leggier, more open habit. CO2 injection is optional but consistently improves density, leaf size and growth rate — some sources list it as required for a tight carpet, which is reasonable for dense aquascapes but overstated for general use. A nutrient-rich substrate (or inert sand/gravel supplemented with root tabs every 2-3 months) is preferred since S. repens is a moderate root feeder; fine substrates also help the small roots penetrate easily. To encourage lateral spreading rather than upright growth, trim the longest vertical shoots after planting — the plant responds by producing new horizontally creeping shoots from the base. Plant in small groups spaced 2-3 cm apart for cohesive carpets. Source spread on parameters: temperature 20-30°C across sources (averaging to ~22-28°C), pH tolerance 5.0-8.0 with ~6.0-7.5 ideal, gH 1-10 dGH, kH ~3-10 dKH. Avoid drastic parameter swings.
breedingNotes: Propagates vegetatively through lateral side shoots and stem cuttings. Trim the longest upright shoots and replant the cuttings, or simply let the plant send out horizontally creeping shoots from the base that root where they touch the substrate. Potted nursery specimens can also be divided into 2-4 pieces at planting. Sexual (flower-based) propagation is not relevant in aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Tropica species article (Staurogyne repens, catalogued as Tropica 049B). Used for: native range (Rio Cristalino, Mato Grosso, southern Amazonas), compact-and-bushy stature distinct from Hygrophila, medium-to-high lighting preference, CO2 preferred, slow growth rate relative to Hygrophila, nutrient-rich substrate recommendation, propagation by division and horizontally creeping shoots, and the popularising-by-Tropica historical context. The direct plantdetails URL returned empty body via WebFetch; the articles URL surfaced the full care content.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Brazil's Rio Cristalino, southern Amazonas), foreground-to-midground placement, low-to-moderate lighting with denser growth under stronger light, CO2 not required but beneficial, medium growth rate, easy care level, temperature 68-82°F (20-28°C), and propagation via trimming longest shoots producing new horizontally creeping shoots from the base.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family Acanthaceae, South American origin, foreground/midground placement, pH 6.0-7.5, and propagation via stem cuttings. Buce lists CO2 as required and care as medium-to-difficult — both stricter than other sources; averaged across all sources, optional CO2 and beginner-to-intermediate care are more accurate for general keeping (this entry uses optional/beginner with the stricter view documented here for reviewer context).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked family Acanthaceae, height 5-10 cm (2-4 in), moderate-to-high lighting at ~10 hours photoperiod, CO2 helpful but not required, slow growth, easy care level, temperature 68-86°F (20-30°C), pH 6-8, kH 3-10, and substrate preference for small grains/sand for root penetration.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (Aquariumbreeder)
additional.notes: Cross-checked family Acanthaceae, native range Rio Cristalino in Mato Grosso, height 8-10 cm, medium-to-high lighting at 30-50 PAR, CO2 not strictly needed, slow growth, easy-to-medium care, temperature 22-30°C, pH optimal 6.0-7.0 (tolerates 5.0-8.0), gH 1-10, minimum 10 gal (~40 L) tank, propagation by side shoots and vegetative buds plus stem cuttings.
